comments.text: Here is a heuristic argument to suggest that the answer should be "yes". Fix $k$, take a random number $n \in [x,2x]$ and heuristically compute the probability that $q(n,k) \geq (1+\varepsilon) \log x$ for some fixed $\varepsilon > 0$. All the primes up to $(1+o(1)) \log x$ will have to divide one of $n+1,\dots,n+k$, so there are $k^{(1+o(1))(\log x/\log\log x)}=x^{(\log k+o(1))/\log\log x}$ ways to allocate these factors. By the Chinese remainder theorem and prime number theorem, this will determine $n$ uniquely. The "probability" that the primes from $(1+o(1)) \log x$ up to $(1+\varepsilon) \log x$ also divide one of $n+1,\dots,n+k$ is heuristically of the shape $(k /(1+O(\varepsilon))\log x)^{(1+o(1))\varepsilon \log x /\log\log x} = x^{-\varepsilon+o(1)}$, which should eventually overwhelm the $x^{(\log k+o(1))/\log\log x}$ factor and suggests that there are only finitely many $n$ with this property. But it looks quite hard to turn this into a rigorous argument! Another trivial remark: the difficulty of this problem is monotone in $k$, so one can focus initially on the $k=2$ case if one is angling for a proof. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
